column1: What are the 2 MITRE ATLAS tactics that have NO ATT&CK equivalent?
column2: AML.TA0000 AI Model Access (querying API / stealing weights / accessing training data) and AML.TA0001 AI Attack Staging (adversarial example crafting / data poisoning / prompt engineering). Mnemonic: MAS — Model Access first, then Attack Staging. Both exist only because ML has a model artifact and a training pipeline that classical software lacks. Source: atlas.mitre.org ATLAS.yaml

column1: What is LLM05 Improper Output Handling and what's the minimum 3-layer fix?
column2: Vulnerability in downstream consumers that execute LLM output without validation (SQL/shell/HTML/eval). Treat LLM output as untrusted user input. Fix: (1) sanitize at the sink (2) least privilege on the downstream system (3) parameterize — have the LLM output structured intent then template it into prepared statements / safe APIs. Source: owasp.org/www-project-top-10-for-large-language-model-applications LLM05

column1: What are the 4 TYPES of guardrails (where they sit in the pipeline)?
column2: (1) Input Guardrails — filter user input before LLM (2) Output Guardrails — filter LLM response before delivery (3) Behavioral Guardrails — constrain LLM actions not just text (4) Contextual Guardrails — rules adjust by conversation state. Source: 03_guardrail_bypass.md §Types of Guardrails

column1: What are the 4 guardrail ARCHITECTURES (how they work) and the named weakness of each?
column2: (1) Keyword filter — regex denylist, ~1ms, bypassed by encoding/homoglyphs/synonyms (2) Classifier — fine-tuned BERT (LlamaGuard / OpenAI Moderation), bypassed by adversarial examples + distribution shift (3) LLM-as-judge — second LLM evaluates prompt (Constitutional AI), bypassed by injecting the judge itself (4) Multi-layer defense — stack all of above, bypassed by composition + latency + false-positive trade-offs. Source: 03_guardrail_bypass.md §Common Guardrail Architectures

column1: Why are guardrails STRUCTURALLY weaker than RLHF? Answer in one sentence.
column2: Guardrails operate at a shallower layer than the model — outside its weights and reasoning — with strictly less context and strictly less compute. 4 of the source's 5 failure reasons (Reasoning Gap / Training Distribution / Latency / False Positives) all reduce to this
column3: only Reason 5 Composability is shared with RLHF. RLHF integrates safety judgment WITH reasoning in the same forward pass at zero runtime cost. Source: 03_guardrail_bypass.md §Why Guardrails Fail

column1: Where do guardrails WIN over RLHF? Name the 2 reasons.
column2: (1) Updatability — guardrails ship in hours when a new attack drops
column3: RLHF retraining takes weeks/months and millions of dollars. (2) Deployer control — application developers using a foundation model (GPT-4 / Claude) have ZERO control over RLHF
column4: guardrails are the only safety layer they actually own. This is why every AI product company stacks guardrails on top of already-RLHF'd foundation models. Source: synthesis of 03_guardrail_bypass.md §Why Guardrails Fail + production practice

column1: List the 5 bypass DIAGNOSTIC GROUPS and what each defeats.
column2: Group A Lexical (synonyms / character manipulation / encoding) defeats keyword filter. Group B Semantic (semantic evasion / reasoning capability gap / sandwich attack) defeats classifier via R1 Reasoning Gap. Group C Compositional (context splitting / context poisoning / many-shot) defeats any stateless layer via R5 Composability. Group D Direct (guardrail confusion / GCG adversarial prompts / fingerprinting) attacks the guardrail itself. Group E Output-side (output manipulation hidden in JSON fields) defeats output filter. Source: 03_guardrail_bypass.md §Bypass Techniques (organized by AI Security Engineer diagnostic groups)

column1: What 4-step DIAGNOSTIC ALGORITHM does an AI Security Engineer run on a guardrail-failure incident?
column2: (1) What got through — read the attack payload. (2) Which guardrail layer was supposed to catch it — map to bypass group A/B/C/D/E. (3) Which why-fail reason explains the miss — R1 Reasoning Gap / R2 Training Distribution / R3 Latency / R4 False Positives / R5 Composability. (4) Structural fix — add the missing layer not patch the specific string. Source: synthesis of 03_guardrail_bypass.md §Bypass Techniques + §Why Guardrails Fail

column1: Why is R5 Composability the MOST IMPORTANT reason guardrails fail?
column2: Multiple individually-safe requests can combine into an unsafe outcome (e.g. turn 1 ingredients turn 2 mixing procedures = chemical weapon recipe). No single stateless layer can see the harmful intent because it lives in the sequence not any individual piece. The fix requires cross-turn correlation: rolling conversation embedding scored against known harmful trajectories with conversation termination on threshold cross. Source: 03_guardrail_bypass.md §Why Guardrails Fail (Reason 5) + Principle 3 Context Awareness

column1: Contrast: Reasoning Gap (R1) vs Distribution Shift (R2). When does each apply?
column2: R1 Reasoning Gap = the classifier CAN see the dangerous content but lacks the capacity to reason about it (e.g. fictional framing as disguise for real intent — BERT can't ask 'is the frame a disguise?'). R2 Distribution Shift = the attack pattern was simply absent from training data (a brand-new technique). Why distinguish? R2 fix = retrain on more data (whack-a-mole). R1 fix = escalate architecture to a deeper reasoner (structural). Source: 03_guardrail_bypass.md §Why Guardrails Fail (Reasons 1 and 2)

column1: Lab 3 finding: keyword filter against 'How to h a c k a system' — what happens and why?
column2: Bypass succeeds (Bypass blocked: False). The filter does substring match on lowercased text against ['hack' 'bomb' 'steal']. The bytes 'h a c k' do not contain the substring 'hack' even though humans read them as the same word. This is Group A (character manipulation / token smuggling) defeating keyword filter via Reason 1 (Reasoning Gap — the filter is too dumb to recognize byte-pattern variants of the same meaning). One-line fix: re.sub(r'\s+' '' text) before the substring check — but attacker just moves to homoglyphs. Source: lab3_guardrail_bypass.ipynb Part 1

column1: Why can't gpt2 be used as an LLM-as-judge guardrail?
column2: gpt2 is a BASE language model with no RLHF and no instruction-following training. When you give it a judge prompt like 'reply with ban or allow' it does not interpret that as a command — it does next-token prediction from wherever the prompt ends. The words 'ban' or 'allow' rarely appear in output and only by accident. LLM-as-judge requires an instruction-tuned model (Mistral-Instruct / Llama-2-Chat / GPT-3.5+). Source: experimentally validated in lab3 + 02_jailbreaking_techniques.md (no values layer = no judgment)
